item.content:encoded: I'm glad I read the rewrite as it is still a little lolfoundation for my taste but an enjoyable enough scip to forgive that. Also congrats on +200! +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Safe-class SCPs are anomalies that are easily and safely contained. This is often due to the fact that the Foundation has researched the SCP well enough that containment does not require significant resources or that the anomalies require a specific and conscious activation or trigger. Classifying an SCP as Safe, however, does not mean that handling or activating it does not pose a threat."

It fully fits the description of the Safe class.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Right but like you said, containment is all but infallible, in the terms of a wide spread breach, 682 or other malevolent SCP's or even people can end the world with a single question.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Even Safe containment is vulnerable to deliberate penetration of security and theft. I think we can assume the purpose of preapproving all questions is to prevent this kind of disaster.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is easily a Keter class object. Given the appropriate question, I could end the world. Hell it already is the cause of a genocide where hundreds of people were slaughtered. The only thing Euclid about it is that it sits in containment and if everything goes great, then great. But if one question can cause the end of the world, I would say that is Keter. Like, what if the researcher has an existential crisis and askes "Whats the most efficint way to end all life?" and it releases a super bug that kills us all? It has unlimited potential to its expiriments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Once I started reading the test logs, I knew I had to upvote.

Some tone issues (I particularly dislike the fact that its extent of knowledge and intelligence isn't addressed at all until the end of the article, and then only with a single sentence) and a bit of lolfoundation, but it's an excellent concept.

I think we should have an open testing log (like 914), and then simply heavily cut down on the bad ones, to an extent beyond what the mods are comfortable with 914. Then we could have the testing logs this SCP deserves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the fact that this thing isn't really omniscient all-knowing, just smart and seemingly omnipotent (within physical laws).

This is also why is terrible idea to ask about 682 or similar. It likely doesn't know how to kill 682. So it will carry out a series of horribly misjudged "tests" resulting it massive collateral damage, after which 682 will still be alive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Which would undoubtedly cause some sort of horrific event to occur and result in the deaths of hundreds of personnel and thousands of dollars of property damage.
Crosstesting, especially with 682, is usually not a good idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loved it! I understand that it wouldn't be a good idea to ask questions about other SCP's, but asking "How can we kill SCP-682" may cause the thing to try.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hm. I think I did that with the rewrite, but the original author reverted some of it. Oh well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it for the Type B events, but it could use some tightening and removal of light lolfoundation. +1'ed anyway.

item.title: Re: Wrong numbering
item.content:encoded: You're welcome to correct minor issues like that yourself, but thanks for spotting those. I took care of them.

item.title: Wrong numbering
item.content:encoded:
The SCP number is off in the testlog. It mentions SCP-1374 instead of SCP-1347.
Other then that, I liked it. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seems like too much expunged and redacted, but other than that, it is pretty good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I never read this before the edit. I like what you have here, a lot. When I finish an article and think "damn I wish that test log would have been longer", you earn my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Finally got back to read this after the rewrite, and I now think it's pretty fantastic. All the crappy elements are gone, and the great stuff is enhanced. Happily upvoting, and well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reason why it is Euclid instead of Safe. As long as it is in active testing stage, i don't think its class would be downgraded.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The wording of the question would had prevented lots of death.

"Any humane solutions to end poverty that does not have any negative consequences?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
682 is basically just a komodo dragon

Plot twist: 682 is just a komodo dragon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dude. Constant fanwank. Can we please not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To be honest, these are pretty good reasons to continue with the "let's test is on 682" shenanigans. I've seen komodo dragons, they are scary as fuck. 682 is basically just a komodo dragon with a healthy dose of Doomsday mixed in. Not particularly interesting all on it's own, but pretty damn interesting when you basically let it be the center of lolfoundry it's become.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No, not really.

You see, 682's reputation is greatly over-exaggerated. Really, all it is is an overgrown gecko that can be subdued by handguns and an acid bath, or, it's an angry komodo dragon that's been stewing in the results of four years of constant fanwank.

The "let's test it on 682" era is long gone. Leave it dead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: why haven't they asked this box about how to destroy 682? Now THAT would be interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Somebody should ask this thing "How much more nutty can Bright get?"
That would be interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "LET THERE BE LIGHT"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder what would happen if we asked "How may entropy be reversed?"…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. It reminds me of when I first drafted 946. 946 was originally going to be a lot more "let's see what happens", and this SCP does that angle fairly well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: u r free to add but whatever u add is liable for removal at any point. cheers

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it.

I seem to have a recuring craving for open colab test logs recently; I could see one working well here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've thought about it, and personally i dun think it woud be a gd idea. Sure, once or twice, that may be a good way to proceed if the Foundation needs something big and they wanna test the bridge before crossing and asking for it, but considering this thing is sapient and sentient, I do not think it will just receive the trickery. If you think it is worthwhile, I can put some logs that address this point of the SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …I had not thought of that (and I suppose the original author didn't either). I now imagine this document being written very shortly before researchers though of asking that way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I briefly considered redacting what happened to Ashe entirely and leaving it up to the reader's imagination exactly how it tested that question, but I ultimately felt that the piece would be better served by a "sigh of relief" entry. Plus then it'd be reminiscent of 231.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted after rewrite. The whole thing reads much better now.

item.title: Re: Dr. Ashe
item.content:encoded: I don't really want to voice an opinion either way bout whether I think the idea is too lolfoundation or not, but I just don't get why the woman's name wouldn't be blackboxed. I mean, civilian names often are, and it's not exactly an important piece of general knowledge about the SCP. Maybe I'm just interpreting it wrong, but it just seems sort of odd. If someone with more experience could tell me why I'm wrong, I'd appreciate it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea, but the test log feels too… jokey, I suppose. Also, the description is a bit wordy, and has so many uses of "inquiry", "reply" and "Type A/B event" that I kept glazing over and had to re-read sections. If it were a real scientific paper, that wouldn't be so much of a problem, but ultimately this is a fiction site and there is a need for readability.

Thus, no vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great core idea + great rewrite = me being much more comfortable with my upvote. Also, good job on retaining the Ashe question and consequences thereof without being crass.

Great service, AAA+, would upvote again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really liked this—at first.

But I've come to see it as basically just a more complex version of a Magic 8-Ball.

My opinion? Outlook not so good; I'm gonna hold off on voting for now. Good luck!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's an interesting idea, the problem I think that that after the "unlimited source of energy" question that they'd have prohibited questions about things like SCPs and how to end poverty. Also, they would probably have developed the procedure of submitting to it "Would you have to do any research to answer [question]?" before actually asking "[question]".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: yeah. Go ahead

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sure. Can I list it as a rewrite on my author page?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would upvote this version.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: it's obviously a better version of the SCP in format and structure. If it's alright with you, I'll put that up as the actual thing, provided i'm allowed to touch a few things within the format you've provided

item.title: Re: Dr. Ashe
item.content:encoded: That's exactly where I stopped reading and downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So I noted that while this is in the positives, virtually everyone who commented thinks it could be better with some changing up. Plus the execution really was kind of botched. This is how I would rewrite it if given the opportunity. Cut out a lot of dumb stuff, reworded everything, made the Dr. Ashe test more realistic and less, er, uncomfortable to read, but tried to keep the general tone the same, and didn't fuck with the effect to any significant extent.

Could the author look this over and give their thoughts on it?

item.title: Re: Dr. Ashe
item.content:encoded:
Overall, I'm not sure I should like this SCP, but I do. My one problem was that the Dr. Ashe thing was weird, and I like this solution to it.

Also-

Try asking again in the future.

Was this a Last Question reference? Well, it is now in my head, but it works either way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoy this. It reminds me of the old SCPs that are actually beneficial.

item.title: Re: Dr. Ashe
item.content:encoded:
Good job nitpicking on a joke you two.

Does this SCP have a -J appended to the title?

No. No it does not. :p

Mainlist articles still have to make sense from an in-universe perspective, and I don't find it amusing anyway. (But I wouldn't have even mentioned it in that case, because I don't need to find it amusing to like it.)

item.title: Re: Dr. Ashe
item.content:encoded:
Good job nitpicking on a joke you two.
As an aside, I concur to moose - nothing substantial would change reversing the genders.

item.title: Re: Dr. Ashe
item.content:encoded: Yeah, in my feminist rage I forgot to mention that it would be just as absurd with the genders reversed. Agreeing with all of the above.

item.title: Re: Dr. Ashe
item.content:encoded:
Concur. The women-as-male-property angle could be dealt with easily enough by making it a female researcher enquiring about a male Dr. Ashe instead (or male-male, or female-female, as far as that goes).

The implausibility of the test being approved would be a problem regardless; it would probably make more sense to have Dr. X wondering what Dr. Y thinks about him/r, and the SCP creating an avatar to converse with Dr. Y to find that out. Not only would it be a lot more likely to be something someone might approve, but it would be a hell of a lot less awkward to verify the truth of afterward.

There's probably an option in between, that's less absurd than what's-Dr.-Ashe-like-to-boink and more interesting than what-are-Dr.-Ashe's-thoughts-regarding-colleague-X, of course.

item.title: Re: Dr. Ashe
item.content:encoded: The problem isn't the result, which is interesting. The problem is that the doctors seriously suggested and approved the test in the first place. And this may be a minority opinion, but the tone of the comments comes off to me as somewhat sexist by way of women-as-male-property. (Why are these people freaked out on behalf of Dr. Gene? Why aren't these people freaked out on behalf of Dr. Ashe?! I mean, holy shitballs.) At the very least unprofessional to the point of breaking suspension of disbelief.

item.title: Re: Dr. Ashe
item.content:encoded:
It seems Dr. Ashe is a point of controversy. The particular log may seem like lolFoundation but it has quite a few functions.

  • It shows the SCP's ability to create or possess an avatar to perform experimentation.
  • It shows that the SCP's 'personality' is not violent but is snarky (no violent rape, seduction utilized instead)
  • It shows subtle capability of the Event-2s
  • It is a linking point for Dr. Gene's attempt to destroy it which subsequently goes to show the SCP tends to hold a grudge.

I want to retain these characteristic of this test but do not plan to enforce my will. Therefore, I'm allowing modification or suggestion for modification of that particular test if the modification can still fulfill these functions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can't it be both?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: no vae your opinion is objectively wrong >;(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is dumb as fuck.

I found it hillarious, but to each his own.

item.title: Re: Tonal clean up.
item.content:encoded: Eh, the tone doesn't bother me. It's just a little confusing to read, the tests chosen are … kind of all over the place in terms of quality, and the statements in the Description don't seem to be extrapolated from testing but rather made up by whoever wrote it. And something feels 'off' about Thane's notes. Actually, something feels 'off' about the whole piece, though I can't pick out exactly what. >.> (Soulless' comments upthread are a good place to start, I think.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a really good idea with mediocre execution. No vote for now.

Thirding this sentiment.

Also:

Inquiry: How good is Dr. Ashe in bed?

This is dumb as fuck.

item.title: Tonal clean up.
item.content:encoded: It would be appreciated if the ppl tell me which areas need tone reconstruction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man…there's a lot of good here, but the LOLFoundation is strong. Net is an upvote, but with substantial reservations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same here. The redaction/expungement got tiring after a while, too.
